Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - AN EFFECTIVE GCN-BASED HIERARCHICAL MULTI - LABEL CLASSIFICATION FOR PROTEIN FUNCTION PRE DICTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We propose an effective method to improve Protein Function Prediction (PFP) utilizing hierarchical features of Gene Ontology (GO) terms. Our method consists of a language model for encoding the protein sequence and a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) for representing Go terms. To reflect the hierarchical structure of GO to GCN, we employ node(GO term)-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information. Our algorithm shows effectiveness in a large-scale graph by expanding the GO graph compared to previous models. Experimental results show that our method outperformed state-of-the-art PFP approaches.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Protein Function Prediction (PFP) is one of the key challenges in the post-genomic era (Zhou et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018). With large numbers of genomes being sequenced every year, the number of novel proteins being discovered is expanding as well (Spalevi´c et al., 2020). On the other side, protein functions are reliably determined in wet-lab experiments which are cumbersome and highcost. As a result, the number of novel protein sequences without function annotations is rapidly expanding. In fact, UniRef100 (Consortium, 2019) contains over 220M (million) protein sequences of which less than 1M have function annotations proved by experiments (Littmann et al., 2021). Fast and accurate PFP is especially important in biomedical and pharmaceutical applications which are associated with specific protein functions.

Protein functions are defined by Gene Ontology (GO) composed of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) (Ashburner et al., 2000). There are three GO domains: Molecular Function Ontology (MFO), Biological Process Ontology (BPO), and Cellular Component Ontology (CCO), where each node represents one function called GO term, and each edge represents a hierarchical relation between two GO terms, such as ’is ~~a~~ ’, ’part ~~o~~ f’, and etc. Since one protein is usually represented by multiple function annotations, PFP can be regarded as hierarchical multi-label classification (HMC).

There are two groups of existing approaches for PFP: local approaches and global approaches. Local approaches usually constructed a classifier for each label (GO term) or for a few labels of the same hierarchy level (Lobley et al., 2008; Minneci et al., 2013; Cozzetto et al., 2016; Rifaioglu et al., 2019). On the other hand, global approaches constructed a single classifier for multiple labels. The initial global approaches considered PFP to flat multi-label classification, ignoring the hierarchical structure of GO, and considering each label independently (Kulmanov & Hoehndorf, 2020). Recent global approaches have constructed a structure encoder to learn the correlation among labels (Zhou et al., 2020; Cao & Shen, 2021). However, these results showed that existing global approach-based models had limitations in representing correlations between GO terms by learning a large-scale hierarchical graph of GO.

One of the structure encoders in global approach-based models used Graph Convolutional Network (GCN). GCN has been applied in learning representation of node features. Nevertheless, in the case of applying GCN to a large-scale hierarchical graph, it was difficult to obtain information among long-distance (Chen et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2021) and unable to obtain adequate structure information since adjacent nodes did not contain any hierarchical features (Hu et al., 2019). To overcome these shortcomings, we build node-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information, which is involved relationship between long-distance nodes and structure information.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

In this paper, we propose a novel PFP model that combines a pre-trained Language Model (LM) and GCN-based model including new node-wise representations. A pre-trained LM as a sequence encoder (Littmann et al., 2021) extracts general ad helpful sequence features. GCN-based model as a structure encoder blends hierarchical information into a graph representation to improve GCN performance in a large-scale hierarchical graph of GO. To predict the probability of each GO term representing target protein functions, the prediction layer is constructed as a dot product of outputs of two encoders. The experimental results show that our method achieves performance improvement compared to the-state-of-the art models, especially in the most difficult BPO.

## 2 RELATED WORK

## 2.1 PROTEIN SEQUENCE FEATURE EXTRACTION

Protein sequences contain multiple biophysical features related to function and structure. Initially, protein biophysical features such as motifs, sequence profiles, and secondary structures were calculated from a suite of programs and then utilized as protein sequence feature vectors by combining them (Lobley et al., 2008; Minneci et al., 2013; Cozzetto et al., 2016; Rifaioglu et al., 2019). While these methods intuitively utilized the relationship between protein features and its biological functions, it required deep knowledge of proteomics and had high-cost.

Various deep learning architectures that extract high-level biophysical features of protein sequences have been proposed. Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is one of the architectures as a sequence encoder to learn sequence patterns or motifs that are related to functions (Xu et al., 2020). Therefore, 1D CNN was utilized as effective sequence encoder in previous researches (Kulmanov & Hoehndorf, 2020; Zhou et al., 2020; Kulmanov et al., 2018).

With the advent of transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), which is attention-based model, in Natural Language Processing (NLP), various attention-based LMs were applied to protein sequence embedding (Rao et al., 2019; Vig et al., 2020; Rives et al., 2021; Heinzinger et al., 2019; Elnaggar et al., 2020). As protein sequences can be considered as sentences, these learned the relationship between amino acids constituting the sequence and learned contextual information. SeqVec (Heinzinger et al., 2019) and ProtBert (Elnaggar et al., 2020), which were learned protein sequences using ElMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), showed that these mostly extracted biophysical features of protein structures and functions, such as secondary structures, binding sites, and homology detections.

## 2.2 PROTEIN FUNCTION PREDICTION (PFP)

PFP methods can be categorized into two different approaches which are local and global. Local approaches commonly employed single or few multi-label classifiers to each or few GO terms. These approaches included FFpred (Lobley et al., 2008; Minneci et al., 2013; Cozzetto et al., 2016) and DEEPred (Rifaioglu et al., 2019). FFpred predicted one GO term by multiple Support Vector Machines (SVMs) trained with radial basis function kernels to recognize protein sequence patterns associated with the GO term (Cozzetto et al., 2016). DEEPred created a multi-label classification model using deep neural network for each GO hierarchical level (Rifaioglu et al., 2019). Each model could carry out five GO terms in most labels. Even though this procedure generated 1,101 different models concerning all GO domains, this still needed numerous models for PFP (Rifaioglu et al., 2019). Ultimately, local approaches required expensive costs by training numerous models.

On the other hand, global approaches respectively constructed one classifier model for MFO, BPO, and CCO. The initial global approaches considered PFP as flat multi-label classification. They focused on extracting function-related features from the protein sequence. One of the function-related features is motifs, called sequence patterns. DeepGoPlus (Kulmanov & Hoehndorf, 2020) encoded sequence to extract motifs using 1D CNN and then predicted the probability of annotating each GO term using one fully connected layer. Compared to local approach-based previous methods, DeepGoPlus achieved improved performance in PFP, despite its simple model architecture. This model resulted in poor performance when the number of GO terms increases. The recent global approaches expanded that built a structure encoder to improve performance. In DeepGOA (Zhou et al., 2020), all GO terms were regarded as correlated labels contrary to DeepGoPlus where they were regarded

as independent labels. This enabled GCN to learn more effectively GO terms. They showed improved performance compared to DeepGoPlus, although the same protein sequence encoding was used. They extracted patterns using 1D CNN, but could not extract various features related to functions other than patterns. TALE (Cao & Shen, 2021) implied transformer encoder (Vaswani et al., 2017) for sequence encoder and embedded GO with hierarchical information of each GO term. They learned various and specific features related to function as the transformer encoder. However, this indirectly learned the correlation among GO terms since they did not learn GO itself and just use hierarchical information of each GO term.

One of the common problems of global approaches is that they partially used GO terms. In general, a cut-off criterion in each GO domain was a number of annotations such as 25, 50, and 150. Global approaches, which had the cut-off criterion, utilized about 13% of the total GO terms. Although TALE (Cao & Shen, 2021), which is the latest model, had the cut-off criterion to 1, this still utilized only about 60% GO terms for their model. In this paper, we propose a method that effectively learns the relation between GO terms in an extended data using over 85% GO terms. This model results in similar or higher performance to the latest models used fewer GO terms.

## 3 PROPOSED METHOD

In this section, we describe the details of our model. The overall architecture is shown in Figure 1. This model has two inputs: a protein sequence and the hierarchical graph of GO. A protein sequence is encoded by pre-trained LM and is reduced dimension to the feature vector size of GO respectively. A GO graph is represented to a large-scale adjacency matrix and node-wise feature matrix. These matrices are inputs to GCN for learning the hierarchical representation of GO terms. The prediction layer is built as a dot product of a protein feature vector and a GO terms vector to predict the probability of annotated GO term of each protein sequence. We explain the technicality of these process in this subsection.

## 3.1 PROTEIN SEQUENCE ENCODING

We employ pre-trained LM as a sequence encoder. As we mentioned in related work, pre-trained LM, such as SeqVec (Heinzinger et al., 2019) and ProtBert (Elnaggar et al., 2020), already proved their performance to capture rudimentary features of proteins such as secondary structures, biological activities, and functions (Rives et al., 2021; Vig et al., 2020). Especially, it was showed that SeqVec (Heinzinger et al., 2019) is better than ProtBert (Elnaggar et al., 2020) to extract high-level features related functions for PFP (Littmann et al., 2021). Seqvec (Heinzinger et al., 2019) is utilized as a protein sequence encoder. This makes the various lengths of protein sequences to 1 _×_ 1024 representation vectors with high-level biophysical features. Protein sequence representations are converted to _P ∈_ R[1] _[×][d]_ low-dimensional representation vectors by fully connected layer to combine GO term vectors.

## 3.2 HIERARCHICAL REPRESENTATION OF GO TERM

Initial node features _H_[0] _∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ are represented as a one-hot encoding matrix where the _i_ th row GO term and its ancestors are 1. The GO term Embedding layer reduces dimension by converting sparse matrix to _H_[0] _∈_ R _[N][×][d]_[0] dense matrix for preventing overfitting and reducing training time in GCN. It indicate that node features contain its physical location and conceptual information in a hierarchical graph. _N_ is a number of GO terms(node) and _d_ 0 is a scale of dimension, which is the maximum depth in each domain for containing hierarchical information in the dense matrix.

The adjacency matrix _A ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ contains the relationship between GO terms. When a parent node is _t_ and the children node is _s_ , the adjacency matrix is combined prior probability _P_ ( _Us|Ut_ ) with Information Content(IC) (Song et al., 2013) that measures semantic similarity between _t_ and _s_ . The existing adjacency matrix is usually built by one-hot encoding or the prior probability. The one-hot encoding can not involve any additional information other than connection information between GO terms. The prior probability can involve relational information. Nevertheless, it apply the inveterate label imbalanced problem in the PFP dataset to the adjacency matrix due to being highly dependent on the training dataset. The prediction layer builds a dot product of protein sequence feature vectors and GO feature vectors to finally predict the probability of each GO term for the one protein sequence. We use the sigmoid function in the prediction layer. The prediction layer is defined as follows:

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 DATASET

The CAFA3(Zhou et al., 2019) dataset, which was used in an international protein function prediction competition, was used for our experiment. According to CAFA3, experimental annotations have one of 8 experimental evidence codes: EXP, IDA, IPI, IMP, IGI, IEP, TAS, and IC. The training dataset includes protein sequences with all known experimental annotations before September 2016. The test dataset includes protein sequences with their experimental annotations known between September 2016 and November 2017. The GO dataset is a version of May 31, 2016. This GO dataset is also used in CAFA3. GO dataset has 11,888 GO terms in molecular function(MFO), 30,546 GO terms in biological process(BPO) and 4241 GO terms in cellular component(CCO).

We propagated annotations using the true path rule. For example, if a protein sequence ’P’ has annotation ’A’ and GO term ’B’ is an ancestor of ’A’, protein sequence ’P’ contains annotations both ’A’ and ’B’. Among various type relations, such as ’is ~~a~~ ’, ’part ~~o~~ f’, and etc, we propagated with only an ’is ~~a~~ ’ type relation as ancestor GO term, as same as a CAFA3 assessment tool. We constructed hierarchical graph of GO as input excepting isolated GO term. Eventually, we utilized over 85% of all GO terms in MFO, over 90% of all GO terms in BPO and CCO. As a result, our model utilized about 15% larger GO graph than previous models.

## 4.2 PERFORMANCE ON THE CAFA3

**Experimental settings** We constructed each GO domain’s model since each domain (MFO, BPO, CCO) had a different number of GO terms. Sequence and GO term’s hidden dimensions _d_ were each GO graph’s maximum depth, which was 41 dimensions of MFO, 80 dimensions of BPO, and 54 dimensions of CCO. Although the maximum depth of BPO was 155 since there were only 42 GO terms with a depth of 80 or more, we used a maximum depth of BPO to 80 for efficient training time.

On the other hand, AUPR is used to evaluate classification problems, especially highly imbalancedlabel classification. We used AuPRC on each GO term to evaluate the performance.

Figure 2: The performance of our model against baseline models on the CAFA3

**Baseline Models and Results** We compared our model using baseline models. The baseline models were Naive, DIAMONDScore, DeepGoCNN, and TALE. Naive predicted protein functions using prior probability. DIAMONDScore predicted protein functions based on the sequence similarity measured using the Diamond tool (Buchfink et al., 2015). DeepGoCNN was the most famous sequence-based PFP model, using 1D CNN protein sequence encoder and a flat multi-label classifier (Kulmanov & Hoehndorf, 2020). TALE was the state-of-the-art sequence-based PFP model, using a transformer encoder for protein sequences encoding and embedding GO terms with hierarchical information (Cao & Shen, 2021). Results were shown in Figure 2. Overall, our model achieved the best performance on all domains in AUPR and on biological process (BPO) in _Fmax_ . Our model especially improved from 0.398 to 0.470 on BPO (by 18%) compared to TALE. All the baseline models did not exceed 0.4 in _Fmax_ on BPO, which had the most complex and large-scale hierarchical graph. It proved that our model was effective in a large-scale hierarchical graph, as it showed highly improved performance on BPO.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we proposed an effective GCN-based model to improve the protein function prediction. We build initial node-wise representations involving the whole hierarchical information to overcome the shortcoming of GCN-based model in a large-scale graph. After that, we combined a pre-trained LM and an effective GCN-based model to predict protein function.

Experimental results showed that in _Fmax_ , our model outperformed the related state-of-the-art methods on BPO, which was the highly difficult domain with the most large-scale hierarchical graph. Moreover in AUPR, our model outperformed the related state-of-the-art methods on all domains. We demonstrated that our model was especially effective in a large-scale graph having deep depth.

Learning correlation of GO terms is an important part of PFP since it is the large-scale hierarchical multi-label classification. It is necessary to extract the features of the protein sequence related to

the function from the PFP. We used a pre-trained LM in the sequence encoder part. It can properly extract the features of the protein sequence, but does not train the encoder while training the model. We will work later on sequence encoders that can extract function-related features using different LMs.

## 6 REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

The learning was processed with 10 epochs and 32 batch sizes. We used ReLU activation function, 1e-3 as the learning rate, and Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014). We simulated our model with NVIDIA TITAN Xp. The code for reproducibility is posted at the anonymous GitHub repository : https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Reproducibility-code

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a coherent and well-motivated approach to Protein Function Prediction (PFP) by effectively integrating pre-trained language models for sequence encoding with GCNs for structural representation. The core contribution—addressing the long-range dependency and structural representation limitations of standard GCNs in large-scale DAGs via node-wise hierarchical embeddings—is technically sound and highly relevant to the field. While the introduction sets a clear stage, the manuscript would benefit from tightening the technical terminology; phrases like “node(GO term)-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information” are somewhat vague and require more rigorous mathematical definition to distinguish clearly from existing graph embedding techniques. Furthermore, the survey of related work is comprehensive, though the paper would be bolstered by a more explicit discussion of how its specific "node-wise" strategy differs from recent attention-based or positional encoding solutions for graph neural networks. Overall, the logic is sound, but the transition to the methods section needs to emphasize the novelty of the embedding mechanism to justify the reported performance gains in complex domains like BPO.

comments.title: Underspecified Loss Function and Hierarchical Consistency
comments.quote: We use binary-cross entropy as the loss function since it is binary problem at each GO term.
comments.explanation: The loss function definition is missing and underspecified. While the paper describes binary cross-entropy, it fails to define the equation or explain how hierarchical dependencies (e.g., the 'true path rule') are enforced. It is also unclear if the model accounts for the severe label imbalance inherent in hierarchical multi-label classification.

comments.title: Missing or Ambiguous Definition of Adjacency Matrix Construction
comments.quote: The adjacency matrix is defined as follows: A_{ts} = P(U_s|U_t) * IC(s)
comments.explanation: The formal definition of the adjacency matrix is missing from the text. Furthermore, the provided description implies a dense matrix calculation (applying the formula for all terms $s$ and $t$), which contradicts the hierarchical DAG structure described elsewhere. The lack of clarity on how IC and prior probabilities are normalized or combined into an adjacency matrix is a major omission.

comments.title: Mathematical Errors and Omissions in Metric Definitions
comments.quote: Fmax Where pr(t) and rc(t) is average precision and recall in threshold t.
comments.explanation: The governing equation for the F-measure is missing, and the subsequent notation for the predicted vector is corrupted (e.g., 'tr[] i'). This prevents verification of the reported performance metrics.

comments.title: Inconsistent and Ambiguous Dimensionality Notation
comments.quote: The GO term Embedding layer reduces dimension by converting sparse matrix to H in R[N][x][d] dense matrix
comments.explanation: The manuscript uses inconsistent notation for dimensionality ($d$ vs $d_0$), describes contradictory definitions for the initial node feature matrix $H^{(0)}$ (sometimes $N 	imes N$ sparse, sometimes $N 	imes d$ dense), and employs confusing bracketed formatting for tensor dimensions. This hinders replicability.

comments.title: Unsupported and Confusing Dimensionality Constraint
comments.quote: Protein sequence representations are converted to P in R[1] [x][d] low-dimensional representation vectors
comments.explanation: The mapping of high-dimensional transformer embeddings (e.g., SeqVec) to a small latent dimension $d$ (capped at the max depth of the graph) is poorly justified. The authors do not explain how this severe compression retains sufficient sequence-encoded information for downstream classification.

comments.title: Mathematical Error in Information Content (IC) Formulation
comments.quote: IC(k) = -log (freq(k) + sum(freq(j))) / total_proteins
comments.explanation: The formula provided calculates IC based only on immediate children rather than all descendants. This contradicts the 'true path rule' standard in Gene Ontology, suggesting either an error in the manuscript's formalization or an flawed implementation of the hierarchical property.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Vague Architectural Terminology
comments.quote: To reflect the hierarchical structure of GO to GCN, we employ node(GO term)-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information.
comments.explanation: The authors fail to define what 'node-wise representation... containing the whole hierarchical information' entails. It is unclear if topological features, path-based embeddings, or relative depths are actually injected into the node features, or if this is merely an assumption about the GCN's implicit capabilities.

comments.title: Confusing Reporting of GO Graph Scaling
comments.quote: maximum depth of BPO was 155 since there were only 42 GO terms with a depth of 80 or more
comments.explanation: The authors describe a truncation of BPO to depth 80 for efficiency, but their justification is internally inconsistent regarding the actual depth of the graph and the capacity to represent nodes beyond this limit. The performance impact of this truncation is not addressed.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper presents a coherent and well-motivated approach to Protein Function Prediction (PFP) by effectively integrating pre-trained language models for sequence encoding with GCNs for structural representation. The core contribution—addressing the long-range dependency and structural representation limitations of standard GCNs in large-scale DAGs via node-wise hierarchical embeddings—is technically sound and highly relevant to the field. While the introduction sets a clear stage, the manuscript would benefit from tightening the technical terminology; phrases like “node(GO term)-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information” are somewhat vague and require more rigorous mathematical definition to distinguish clearly from existing graph embedding techniques. Furthermore, the survey of related work is comprehensive, though the paper would be bolstered by a more explicit discussion of how its specific "node-wise" strategy differs from recent attention-based or positional encoding solutions for graph neural networks. Overall, the logic is sound, but the transition to the methods section needs to emphasize the novelty of the embedding mechanism to justify the reported performance gains in complex domains like BPO.

comments.title: OCR-induced typo in description of GO relations
comments.quote: such as ’is ~~a~~ ’, ’part ~~o~~ f’, and etc.
comments.explanation: The text contains artifacts from OCR processing (e.g., 'is a' appearing as 'is ~a~' and 'part of' as 'part ~o~ f'). While the intended meaning is clearly the standard Gene Ontology relations 'is_a' and 'part_of', these artifacts are distracting and technically inaccurate. Since the prompt asks to flag issues regardless of OCR status if they represent a real issue, this should be noted as a minor quality issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimension notation for GO term embeddings
comments.quote: The GO term Embedding layer reduces dimension by converting sparse matrix to $H_{[0]} \in R [N][\times][d]_{[0]}$ dense matrix
comments.explanation: The notation uses subscript indexing $d_{[0]}$ to denote the dimension. However, elsewhere in the text, $d$ is used for hidden dimensions, and standard matrix notation would be $R^{N \times d_0}$. The use of brackets around subscripts ($[0]$) combined with the multiplication symbol representation ([\times]) is highly irregular and likely an OCR misread or non-standard notation that could be confusing to a researcher attempting to implement the architecture.

comments.title: Underspecified Loss Function Definition
comments.quote:
We use binary-cross entropy as the loss function since it is binary problem at each GO term. The prediction layer is defined as follows:

[Equation missing from text]
comments.explanation: While the passage describes using binary cross-entropy, there is a lack of formal definition for the loss function equation. In hierarchical multi-label classification, binary cross-entropy is often applied per node, but there is no mention of how the hierarchy is incentivized or penalized within the loss (e.g., if there are consistency constraints based on the DAG). Given that the paper emphasizes the hierarchical structure, the absence of an explicit loss formulation that incorporates this structure is a major omission.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'node-wise' versus 'graph-wide' representations
comments.quote: To reflect the hierarchical structure of GO to GCN, we employ node(GO term)-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to include 'whole hierarchical information' in 'node-wise representations'. However, in later sections, they describe the adjacency matrix and GCN layers. It is unclear if they are injecting hierarchical structural information (like distance in the DAG, positional encoding, or path-based embeddings) into the initial node features $H_{[0]}$ or if the GCN aggregation is intended to capture this. The term 'node-wise representation' is vague and does not clearly differentiate from standard GCN node features.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in sequence representation
comments.quote: Protein sequence representations are converted to $P \in R[1] [\times][d]$ low-dimensional representation vectors
comments.explanation: The text states the protein representation is converted to a vector of size $1 \times d$, where $d$ is the hidden dimension of the GO terms. However, earlier in the paper, $d$ is defined as the maximum depth of the GO graph (e.g., 41, 80, 54). Mapping a 1024-dimensional transformer embedding (from SeqVec) directly to a very low-dimensional vector (e.g., 41) via a single fully connected layer is a significant dimensionality reduction that likely ignores much of the sequence-encoded information. Furthermore, standard practice in HMC usually maps sequence features to the same space as the node embeddings, but the restriction to 'maximum depth' as the latent dimension size is an unusually tight constraint that is not well-justified.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Missing definition of adjacency matrix calculation
comments.quote:
The adjacency matrix is defined as follows:

Prior probability $P ( U_s|U_t )$ is calculated as follows:
comments.explanation: The text introduces the adjacency matrix definition followed by a heading for the prior probability, but the actual formula for the adjacency matrix (the GCN-ready version combining prior probability and IC) is missing from the document. The text claims to 'solve this problem that adding IC', but never explicitly defines the resulting matrix $A$ in terms of these components (e.g., is it a weighted sum, a normalized product, etc.?). This makes the core 'novel' contribution of the graph construction process underspecified.

comments.title: Incorrect notation for loss function
comments.quote: We use binary-cross entropy as the loss function since it is binary problem at each GO term.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to the problem as 'binary-cross entropy' but describe the task as 'Hierarchical Multi-label Classification' which involves predicting multiple labels simultaneously. While BCE is standard for multi-label tasks, the text implies that each GO term is treated as an independent binary classification. Given the focus on the 'correlation' and hierarchical structure, simply applying standard BCE without a hierarchical penalty (e.g., enforcing consistency that if a child is predicted, the parent must be predicted) is a significant implementation detail that is underspecified, especially given the claim of being a global approach.

comments.title: Inconsistency in dimensionality notation
comments.quote: $H[0] \in R [N][\times][d]_0$ dense matrix ... $H[l] \in R [N][\times][d]$ with node features of adjacent nodes through lth GCN layer
comments.explanation: The text oscillates between $d_0$ (max depth) for the initial layer and $d$ for subsequent layers. Later, in the Experiments section, the authors state they used the maximum depth for both the sequence encoder dimension and the GO term dimension. The notation $d_0$ and $d$ appear to refer to the same value in practice, which is confusing. Additionally, the OCR-induced formatting $R[N][\times][d]_0$ is notationally ambiguous regarding where the indices are placed.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Error in Fmax Definition
comments.quote:
Fmax  

Where pr(t) and rc(t) is average precision and recall in threshold t.
comments.explanation: The text abruptly terminates after 'Fmax' without presenting the formula for the F-measure. While the context provides descriptions of precision and recall, the governing equation for the F-measure itself is missing (likely an OCR artifact or omission). This is a critical mathematical definition for a paper claiming performance based on this metric, making it impossible for a reader to verify the calculation or interpretation of the reported results.

comments.title: Symbol Typo in Equation Variable
comments.quote: tr[] i(t) i-th GO term’s predicted vector at threshold t.
comments.explanation: The symbol 'tr[] i' contains an illegible/corrupted character (likely a misread of 'hat{y}' or similar). Given the subsequent mention of 'i-th GO term’s predicted vector', the notation is inconsistent with the previously defined predicted vector 'hat{y}' in the summary. Without a clear definition of what this symbol represents, the reader cannot replicate the calculation of the F-measure.

comments.title: Inconsistent Dimension Reporting in Methodology
comments.quote: maximum depth of BPO was 155 since there were only 42 GO terms with a depth of 80 or more, we used a maximum depth of BPO to 80 for efficient training time.
comments.explanation: The authors state the sequence and GO term's hidden dimension 'd' is determined by the graph's maximum depth (41, 80, 54). However, the justification for truncating BPO to 80 mentions a maximum depth of 155, yet Table 1 shows BPO has 28,678 GO terms. It is unclear if 'maximum depth' refers to the longest path in the DAG or the number of levels. If the depth is 155, it is confusing why the dimension 'd' would be capped at 80 for 'efficient training time' without explaining the impact on the capacity to represent nodes at depths 81-155.

comments.title: Ambiguous Loss Function Definition
comments.quote: We use binary-cross entropy as the loss function since it is binary problem at each GO term.
comments.explanation: While this is a common practice, the authors do not specify how the positive/negative samples are balanced, especially given the HMC (Hierarchical Multi-label Classification) nature and the 'true path rule' mentioned. In HMC, binary cross-entropy on each node can lead to label imbalance issues. The authors mention 'mitigating label imbalance' via IC in the adjacency matrix but do not clarify if the loss function is adjusted (e.g., weighted BCE) to account for the sparsity of positive labels, which is standard in such models.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in IC definition
comments.quote: IC(k) = -\log \frac{freq(k) + \sum_{j \in child(k)} freq(j)}{total\_proteins}
comments.explanation: The definition provided suggests IC calculation based on the frequency of a node plus its immediate children. However, the standard definition of Information Content in the context of GO DAGs uses the frequency of the node and all of its descendants (the 'true path rule'), because a node is annotated only if one of its descendants is annotated. If only immediate children are summed, as the formula implies, the IC calculation would be conceptually incorrect for the hierarchical properties being claimed. Given the prior context states the hierarchy relies on the 'true path rule', this formula is likely an OCR misread or an error in the manuscript's formalization of IC.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimensionality of initial node features
comments.quote: Initial node features $H^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^{[N][\times][N]}$ are represented as a one-hot encoding matrix where the $i$ th row GO term and its ancestors are 1.
comments.explanation: The text first defines $H^{(0)}$ as having dimensions corresponding to $N \times N$ (a square matrix), but then immediately says: 'The GO term Embedding layer reduces dimension by converting sparse matrix to $H^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^{[N][\times][d]} [0]$ dense matrix'. Using $N \times N$ for a one-hot representation of ancestors is standard, but the text is contradictory regarding which matrix serves as the 'Initial node feature matrix' $H^{(0)}$. $H^{(0)}$ should either be the $N \times N$ sparse matrix or the $N \times d_0$ dense matrix; being identified as both is confusing.

comments.title: Underspecified Adjacency Matrix construction
comments.quote: The adjacency matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{[N][\times][N]}$ contains the relationship between GO terms... $A_{ts} = P(U_s|U_t) \cdot IC(s)$
comments.explanation: While the paper defines $P(U_s|U_t)$ and $IC(s)$, it fails to define the matrix $A$ for cases where $s$ is NOT a child of $t$. Usually, adjacency matrices in GCNs are sparse and only include entries for existing edges. By stating $A_{ts} = P(U_s|U_t) \cdot IC(s)$, the text implies a dense matrix calculation (since $s$ could be any GO term). This would lead to a fully connected graph rather than a hierarchical one, contradicting the paper's emphasis on the GO DAG structure.

comments.title: Claim-result discrepancy regarding model novelty
comments.quote: Our algorithm shows effectiveness in a large-scale graph by expanding the GO graph compared to previous models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that using 15% more GO terms compared to previous models is a key contributor to performance. However, they also state in Section 2.2 that TALE (the state-of-the-art model they compare against) already uses a 'cut-off criterion to 1', meaning TALE effectively considers almost all GO terms. The jump from 'about 60% GO terms' in TALE to 'over 85%' in the proposed method is a notable increase, but claiming this as a primary novelty while the method's architecture (GCN) is the fundamental differentiator is potentially confusing and overstates the impact of the data-filtering change alone.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
**High-Level Feedback**

The paper tackles an important problem—protein function prediction using hierarchical Gene Ontology labels—and proposes a hybrid approach combining a language model for sequence encoding with a graph convolutional network for ontology structure. However, the manuscript in its current form suffers from several clarity and quality issues that must be addressed. The abstract is overly generic, lacking concrete details about the proposed node-wise representations and how they overcome the limitations of standard GCNs on large-scale DAGs. The introduction does not clearly articulate the specific gap in prior work (e.g., why existing GCN-based methods fail for long-range dependencies in GO) or present a concise summary of the paper’s contributions. The related work section reads as a descriptive catalog rather than a critical analysis, with limited discussion of why prior methods are insufficient and how the proposed work differs. Furthermore, the writing contains several typographical and formatting errors (e.g., “Pre diction” in the title, garbled characters in GO relations), which undermine professional presentation. The most significant issue is the lack of any preliminary results or performance comparisons in the introduction or related work, making it difficult to assess the claimed state-of-the-art improvement. The paper would benefit from a sharper problem statement, a clearer explanation of the novel node-wise representation, and a more rigorous evaluation of where previous methods fall short.

comments.title: Contradiction in claim about baseline Fmax on BPO
comments.quote: All the baseline models did not exceed 0.4 in Fmax on BPO
comments.explanation: The table in Figure 2 shows DeepGoCNN (a baseline model) with Fmax 0.411 on BPO, which exceeds 0.4. This directly contradicts the statement. The claim appears to overlook DeepGoCNN's performance, which undermines the comparison and could mislead readers about the relative difficulty of BPO for baselines.

comments.title: Missing equation for adjacency matrix combining prior probability and IC
comments.quote: The adjacency matrix is defined as follows:
comments.explanation: The authors state that the adjacency matrix combines prior probability with Information Content (IC) but never provide the actual formula. Only the definitions of prior probability and IC are given, without specifying how they are combined (e.g., addition, multiplication, weighted sum). This is a core contribution of the method, and its absence makes the approach irreproducible.

comments.title: Missing equation for GCN layer update
comments.quote: GCN layer is represented as follows:
comments.explanation: The GCN update rule is fundamental to the model, yet the equation is not shown. Without specifying the propagation function (e.g., whether symmetric normalization is used, activation function, number of layers M), the method is severely underspecified. This is especially problematic because the paper claims to address limitations of GCN in large-scale graphs.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition and integration of Information Content (IC)
comments.quote: IC is calculated as follows: Where p(k) is probability of each GO term k in the GO dataset, freq(k) is frequency of t and child(k) is every children of k.
comments.explanation: The description of the IC calculation is garbled and incomplete. The variable names are inconsistent (freq(k) is said to be 'frequency of t' instead of k), the formula is omitted, and it is unclear how IC is combined with prior probability in the adjacency matrix. This further contributes to the underspecification of the adjacency matrix.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference in 'recent global approaches expanded that built a structure encoder'
comments.quote: The recent global approaches expanded that built a structure encoder to improve performance.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'expanded that built' is grammatically awkward and unclear. It reads as if 'that' refers to an earlier concept (perhaps the flat multi-label classification or the model architecture), but the intended meaning is likely 'expanded by building a structure encoder' or 'included a structure encoder'. The context later clarifies that DeepGOA and TALE use GCN and transformer-based structure encoders, but the sentence itself is poorly constructed and could confuse a reader about what exactly was expanded. This is a minor writing clarity issue, not a technical error.

comments.title: Incorrect verb tense in 'resulted in poor performance'
comments.quote: This model resulted in poor performance when the number of GO terms increases.
comments.explanation: The sentence mixes past tense ('resulted') with present tense ('increases'). The intended meaning is likely a general observation: DeepGoPlus performs poorly as the number of GO terms increases. The mixed tense is distracting but does not obscure the meaning. It is a minor stylistic inconsistency.

comments.title: Overclaim about 'effectively learn the relation between GO terms in an extended data using over 85% GO terms'
comments.quote: In this paper, we propose a method that effectively learns the relation between GO terms in an extended data using over 85% GO terms. This model results in similar or higher performance to the latest models used fewer GO terms.
comments.explanation: The authors claim 'effectively learns the relation between GO terms' but provide no direct analysis or ablation study demonstrating that the higher GO term coverage is the cause of improved performance. The later experimental results (Figure 2) show improvements on BPO but only marginal or mixed results on MFO and CCO (e.g., Fmax on MFO is 0.518 vs TALE's 0.548, which is worse). The claim that the model 'results in similar or higher performance to the latest models used fewer GO terms' is partially contradicted by the MFO Fmax results. This overstates the evidence, as the method does not uniformly outperform across all metrics and domains. A more precise claim would note domain-specific gains.

comments.title: Missing definition of key variable in adjacency matrix formula
comments.quote: Prior probability P(Us|Ut) is calculated as follows: Where Ut means a number of annotations in the training dataset, P(Us|Ut) means the conditional probability that t and s are annotated in the same protein sequence. IC is calculated as follows: Where p(k) is probability of each GO term k in the GO dataset, freq(k) is frequency of t and child(k) is every children of k.
comments.explanation: The passage states that the adjacency matrix combines prior probability with IC, but the actual formulas for P(Us|Ut) and IC are not shown in the provided text—only textual descriptions are given. The paper later mentions that IC is 'less affected by the training dataset', but the exact mathematical definition of frequency freq(k) and how 'child(k)' is used are left vague. For reproducibility, the reader would need the explicit formulas, which may appear later in the paper (in section 3.2). Since the context cuts off the equations, this is not a definitive error, but it is an underspecified method if the formulas remain absent or ambiguous. I note this as a potential reproducibility concern if the full equations are not provided.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'added IC, which is less affected by the training dataset'
comments.quote: We solve this problem that adding IC, which is less affected by the training dataset (Zhou et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'adding IC' is ambiguous: does it mean the adjacency matrix element is the sum of prior probability and IC, or the product, or a weighted combination? The citation to Zhou et al. (2020) suggests the authors follow a prior method, but the exact integration is not specified in the text. For a reader to understand the novelty and to reproduce the approach, the precise combination rule must be stated. This is an underspecified method detail.

comments.title: Misleading statement about TALE learning 'various and specific features related to function'
comments.quote: TALE (Cao & Shen, 2021) implied transformer encoder (Vaswani et al., 2017) for sequence encoder and embedded GO with hierarchical information of each GO term. They learned various and specific features related to function as the transformer encoder. However, this indirectly learned the correlation among GO terms since they did not learn GO itself and just use hierarchical information of each GO term.
comments.explanation: The authors criticize TALE for only 'indirectly' learning GO term correlations because it uses hierarchical information per term rather than learning GO embeddings directly. However, TALE's method indeed learns joint sequence-label embeddings via a transformer, which can capture label correlations through attention over label embeddings. The claim that it 'did not learn GO itself' is somewhat misleading—TALE does learn label representations, just not via a GCN. The critique is valid that TALE does not use the full GO graph structure, but the phrasing overstates the limitation. This is a framing issue rather than a technical error.

comments.title: Propagation using only 'is_a' relations may not match CAFA3 assessment tool
comments.quote: Among various type relations, such as 'is ~~a~~ ', 'part ~~o~~ f', and etc, we propagated with only an 'is ~~a~~ ' type relation as ancestor GO term, as same as a CAFA3 assessment tool.
comments.explanation: The authors state that they propagated annotations using only 'is_a' relations (not other hierarchical relations like 'part_of') and claim this matches the CAFA3 assessment tool. In standard GO annotation propagation, the true path rule typically applies to all hierarchical relations (is_a, part_of, regulates, etc.), not just is_a. The CAFA3 evaluation likely uses all relations as well. If the authors used only is_a while CAFA3 uses all, then their graph construction and annotation propagation differ from the assessment tool, potentially affecting comparability and the validity of their claim of matching the tool. This requires verification or clarification. The intent seems to be to simplify, but the justification is questionable.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about '15% larger GO graph'
comments.quote: Eventually, we utilized over 85% of all GO terms in MFO, over 90% of all GO terms in BPO and CCO. As a result, our model utilized about 15% larger GO graph than previous models.
comments.explanation: The paper earlier states that previous models used about 13% or 60% of GO terms. Moving from 60% to 85% is a 25 percentage point increase, not 15%. The phrase '15% larger' is ambiguous: it could mean 15 percentage points larger (not conventional) or 15% relative increase. A 15% relative increase from 60% would be about 69%, not 85%. This inconsistency could mislead readers about the extent of graph expansion. The authors likely intended to say that the graph is about 15 percentage points larger, but the wording '15% larger' is imprecise.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript proposes a hybrid architecture integrating pre-trained language models and Graph Convolutional Networks for Protein Function Prediction, addressing a relevant problem within a rapidly evolving and saturated research area. While the motivation to enhance graph encoding by incorporating global hierarchical information is sound, the paper's quality is compromised by significant clarity issues and a lack of technical precision. The core contribution, described as "node-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information," is articulated vaguely; the text outlines the desired outcome (capturing long-range dependencies and structural features) but omits critical details on the specific computational mechanism, making it difficult to assess the novelty of this component relative to established techniques such as graph attention mechanisms or global context aggregation methods. Furthermore, the manuscript contains distracting editorial errors, including typographical mistakes and unrendered LaTeX artifacts (e.g., "ad helpful," "is ~~a~~ ' ' part ~~o~~ f"), which undermine professionalism. Given the prevalence of LM-GCN combinations in bioinformatics, the authors must provide a much sharper definition of their architectural innovation and a rigorous comparison against a comprehensive set of recent baselines to substantiate the claims of state-of-the-art performance.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for initial features and embedding dimensions
comments.quote: Initial node features H[0] ∈ R[N][×][N] are represented as a one-hot encoding matrix... converting sparse matrix to H[0] ∈ R[N][×][d][0] dense matrix... Initial node features H[0] are updated H[l] ∈ R[N][×][d] with node features of adjacent nodes... d 0 is a scale of dimension, which is the maximum depth in each domain...
comments.explanation: The manuscript reuses H^{[0]} for two fundamentally different matrices: the initial N×N sparse one-hot ancestor matrix and the subsequent N×d_0 dense embedded features. This creates a direct notation conflict. Additionally, the dimension symbol switches from d_0 for the initial embedding to d for the updated features H^{[l]} without explanation, implying an undocumented projection. Furthermore, defining d_0 as strictly equal to the maximum hierarchical depth of the GO domain is non-standard; embedding dimensions are typically hyperparameters chosen based on model capacity, not graph depth. These inconsistencies confuse the architectural flow and should be clarified with distinct symbols and standard hyperparameter notation.

comments.title: Underspecified adjacency matrix fusion and ambiguous prior probability notation
comments.quote: the adjacency matrix is combined prior probability P(Us|Ut) with Information Content(IC)... Where Ut means a number of annotations in the training dataset, P(Us|Ut) means the conditional probability that t and s are annotated in the same protein sequence.
comments.explanation: Two interrelated issues affect the graph construction. First, the method for combining prior probability P(U_s|U_t) and Information Content (IC) into the adjacency matrix A is underspecified. The text mentions 'combined' and 'adding IC' but never provides the mathematical operation (e.g., weighted sum, product, normalization), which is critical for reproducibility. Second, U_t is defined as a 'number of annotations' (a scalar), yet it is used as the conditioning event in P(U_s|U_t). This creates a category error; U_t should denote the set of proteins annotated with term t (or the event itself), with its cardinality used in the probability ratio. Clarifying the fusion formula and correcting the set/count notation is essential.

Information Content in Gene Ontology depends on the term's own annotation frequency (or its descendants), not a parent term t. This should read 'frequency of k'. As written, it breaks the logical consistency of the IC formula and would mislead implementation.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel approach to Protein Function Prediction by integrating a pre-trained language model with a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) designed to leverage the hierarchical structure of the Gene Ontology (GO) graph. The introduction is clear and effectively situates the work within the context of the bioinformatics challenges of the post-genomic era. However, the most significant issue facing the reviewers is the lack of technical specificity regarding the core novelty: the claim of using "node(GO term)-wise representations containing the whole hierarchical information." This description is too abstract to judge the contribution; reviewers need to understand whether this involves a novel graph construction technique (e.g., expanding the GO graph, adding synthetic edges, or using a specific hierarchical encoder), a distinct variation of attention mechanisms, or simply a standard pooling strategy. Additionally, the paper implies a distinct advantage over existing global approaches that failed to capture long-distance correlations, yet standard GCNs are computationally capable of this; it is unclear if the method introduces a fundamental architectural shift or simply applies existing architectures to a modified graph, which reduces the perceived novelty for a top-tier venue like ICLR.

The intended meaning is that the LM extracts features that are both general and helpful, but the current text implies an abbreviation or adjective 'ad' which is unclear.

comments.explanation: The term 'protBert' is written in all lowercase, while its counterparts 'SeqVec', 'ElMo', and 'BERT' are consistently capitalized. This inconsistency suggests a typo or a formatting error. For a professional academic presentation, acronyms and proper nouns should be capitalized or follow a uniform style (e.g., Title Case or Sentence case).

comments.title: Logical / Phrasing Issue: 'which were learned protein sequences using'
comments.quote: SeqVec (Heinzinger et al., 2019) and ProtBert (Elnaggar et al., 2020), which were learned protein sequences using ElMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2018)
comments.explanation: The sentence structure creates a logical confusion regarding the subjects and the objects. The phrase 'which were learned protein sequences' implies that the models themselves (SeqVec and ProtBert) were the learned sequences, or it incorrectly assigns the transitive action of 'learning a protein sequence' to the models in a way that obscures that the models are used to *process* or *embed* sequences rather than 'being learned sequences'. It is grammatically awkward and semantically imprecise.

comments.title: Ambiguity: 'carry out five GO terms'
comments.quote: Each model could carry out five GO terms in most labels.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'carry out five GO terms' is ambiguous. It could mean exactly five terms were handled, or it is a mistranslation for 'carry out predictions for up to five', 'score five', or 'handle prediction for five'. In the context of multi-label classification (PFP) where proteins often have hundreds of annotations, specifying exactly 'five' is a potential over-specific claim or a vague generalization that misleads the reader about the model's capacity.

comments.title: Redundancy: 'expensive costs'
comments.quote: Ultimately, local approaches required expensive costs by training numerous models.
comments.explanation: The adjective 'expensive' redundantly describes the noun 'costs'. It is sufficient to say 'required high costs' or 'required significant resources'. While not a technical error, this phrasing can feel clunky in an academic writing style.

comments.title: Overloaded Notation for Initial Node Features
comments.quote: Initial node features $H^{(0)} \in R^{N \times N}$ are represented as a one-hot encoding matrix... The GO term Embedding layer reduces dimension by converting sparse matrix to $H^{(0)} \in R^{N \times d_0}$ dense matrix
comments.explanation: The paper uses the symbol $H^{(0)}$ to define the dimensionality of the initial node features ($N \times N$) and then later uses it again to define the dimensionality after the embedding layer ($N \times d_0$). Since $d_0$ is defined as the 'scale of dimension... which is the maximum depth' and $N$ is the number of terms, these dimensions are distinct. Using the same symbol for two mathematically different matrices ($R^{N \times N}$ vs $R^{N \times d_0}$) creates ambiguity regarding the data flow through the model.

comments.explanation: The text contains multiple forward references to mathematical formulas ('is calculated as follows', 'is represented as follows', 'is defined as follows'), but the actual equations, matrices, or formulas are missing from the provided passage. Key steps such as the definition of the prior probability $P(U_s|U_t)$, the calculation of Information Content (IC), the GCN layer update rule, and the prediction layer function are referenced but not formally defined, making the methodology difficult to verify or reproduce from this text alone.
